title: The Vic Allen Dinner

jokes.text: What rhymes with majority? Nothing. / Oh, priority. And authority. Sorority. Oh, my God, there's a million words that rhyme with it.
jokes.setup: Dan claims nothing rhymes with 'majority' while working on a comedy song
jokes.punchline: Mike and others immediately rattle off multiple obvious rhymes, exposing Dan's incompetence
jokes.explanation: Quick, functional cold open gag that establishes the song-writing subplot while poking fun at Dan's arrogance masking incompetence. The rapid-fire correction from multiple voices is well-paced but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Jonah finishes his sentence about what kind of person would be jealous — 'I don't know, dick, I guess' — delivered as a talking head cut away after a beat of silence.
jokes.setup: Jonah begins a sentence implying Dan would have to be a shallow kind of person to be jealous of a parking spot
jokes.punchline: Cut to Jonah alone in confessional completing the sentence with 'dick, I guess' — the cut itself is the joke, a classic Veep mockumentary beat
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke — the scene break IS the punchline. The dangling incomplete sentence, resolved in confessional as 'dick, I guess,' is perfectly economical. The cut timing and the casual delivery of the insult make it punch above its weight. Justifies the 8+ structure score: the form and content are inseparable and it couldn't land any other way.

jokes.text: Look at the size of his coffee mug. Jeez, you could hide a baby in that thing.
jokes.setup: Selina reviews the situation room photo for release
jokes.punchline: The mug is so large you could hide a baby in it — absurd scale comparison
jokes.explanation: Cheerful, throwaway visual gag. The 'hide a baby in it' image is funny but not particularly crafted — it's a stock 'it's so big that...' construction. Works as character texture for Selina's cattiness.

jokes.text: You look concerned, but steely. Like a lady soldier.
jokes.setup: Gary compliments Selina on how she looks in the photo
jokes.punchline: 'Lady soldier' is meant as a sincere compliment from Gary, which is funnier than any deliberate insult — the specificity of the image is gloriously absurd
jokes.explanation: Pure Gary — the joke only works because of his sycophantic sincerity. 'Lady soldier' as a compliment is both absurd and entirely in character. Strong character integration score because no one else on the show would land this line the same way.

jokes.text: Oh, there are not gonna be any veterans at this teen prayer breakfast thing tomorrow, are there? No, ma'am. No missing legs.
jokes.setup: Selina asks nervously about veterans at the event
jokes.punchline: Gary reassures her with 'No missing legs' — reducing the concern to its bluntest, most clinical form
jokes.explanation: Dark, efficient Veep joke. 'No missing legs' is the kind of bluntly reductive reassurance that only Gary would deliver with total sincerity. Perfectly economical — three words that sum up an entire anxious calculation these characters are making. Earns its craft score for economy and the dark edge.

jokes.text: Who the hell is religious as a teenager? I was. I mean, smoke some weed, for Christ's sake, right?
jokes.setup: Selina dismisses religious teenagers while preparing for a prayer breakfast
jokes.punchline: She says 'for Christ's sake' — taking the Lord's name in vain while mocking the religious — the irony is self-undermining
jokes.explanation: Selina's hypocrisy is on full display — mocking religious teens while accidentally invoking Christ. The self-undermining irony is the engine. The setup also lands a beat on Gary ('I was') that deepens the scene's texture.

jokes.text: It's like two little clutch bags attached to his face.
jokes.setup: They're mocking the president's jowls in the photo
jokes.punchline: Gary reduces the president's face to an accessory metaphor, consistent with his bag-obsession character trait
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character integration makes this joke sing. Gary's obsession with bags is a known character trait, and the show weaponizes it here — the president's jowls become 'clutch bags.' The joke would be funny from anyone, but from Gary it's brilliant. 'Little clutch bags attached to his face' is highly quotable and the image is perfect. Justifies 9.0 on character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, with the bags. It's always bags with you. Got to get out of the bag zone.
jokes.setup: Gary just compared the president's jowls to clutch bags
jokes.punchline: Selina calls out Gary's pathological bag fixation with 'Got to get out of the bag zone'
jokes.explanation: The callback to Gary's bag fixation lands well because Selina's self-awareness about Gary's quirk is funny. 'Bag zone' is a good coinage. Works as a coda to joke 7 rather than a standalone, which is appropriate — it earns slightly less than the setup.

jokes.text: We're all being hit by deadlines, right, Mike? Yes, sir. They can swing around and smack you like a boom.
jokes.setup: Kent shuts down Dan's attempt to show strategy ideas, and Dan deflects by commiserating about deadlines with Mike
jokes.punchline: Mike's nautical metaphor 'like a boom' is a non-sequitur sycophantic reach — and then Kent literally has a boat, making it accidentally apt
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — Mike's desperate grasping for a metaphor that accidentally becomes a sailing reference works as mild character comedy about his fawning tendencies. The payoff that Kent actually sails makes the preceding awkward metaphor retroactively funnier on rewatch.

jokes.text: Leaning out in the tack, your big man balls dipping in the salty sea.
jokes.setup: Kent is rhapsodizing about the joys of catamaran sailing
jokes.punchline: The robotic Kent suddenly deploys graphic anatomical imagery with zero irony or self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The joke's entire power comes from character integration — Kent is the least likely character to produce the phrase 'big man balls dipping in the salty sea,' and that incongruity is the engine. Delivered with deadpan sincerity, it's a perfect subversion of his robotic persona. High quotability because the phrase itself is so unexpected from this character.

jokes.text: I have no children and I wish for none, but she's my family.
jokes.setup: Kent speaks tenderly about his boat
jokes.punchline: His explicit clarification that he wishes for no children, delivered with the same affect as his boat sentiment, reveals the depth of his sociopathy
jokes.explanation: The casual, unprompted parenthetical 'and I wish for none' is the joke. Nobody asked. The fact that Kent needed to add that clarification tells you everything about his inner life. Excellent economy — five unprompted words of character revelation. Earns its craft scores.

jokes.text: You know, I once went powerboating on Lake Erie. Amazing rush.
jokes.setup: Kent and Mike are bonding intensely over sailing as a serious pursuit
jokes.punchline: Dan attempts to join in with powerboating on Lake Erie — the most unsophisticated, unromantic version of water recreation possible — and calls it an 'amazing rush'
jokes.explanation: Dan's inability to read the room is the running joke of this scene. 'Lake Erie' is the perfect choice — it's the butt of Great Lakes jokes, the most utilitarian and unglamorous body of water. 'Amazing rush' doubles down on his tone-deafness. Then the cold reaction from the sailors makes it land.

jokes.text: You don't talk powerboating to us sailors, Dan.
jokes.setup: Dan has just mentioned powerboating on Lake Erie
jokes.punchline: Kent delivers a withering dismissal with the gravity of a statesman rebuking an ally
jokes.explanation: Kent's delivery makes this. 'You don't talk powerboating to us sailors' has the cadence of a diplomatic rebuke — it sounds like he's saying 'You don't talk tariffs to us, sir.' The weight he puts on 'us sailors' is perfect. Highly quotable because of its absurd portentousness. Excellent economy. Justifies the 8+ scores.

jokes.text: What's the fucking difference?
jokes.setup: Kent has just gravely dismissed Dan's powerboating
jokes.punchline: Dan's bewildered, aggrieved response reveals he genuinely doesn't understand the social hierarchy he just violated
jokes.explanation: The perfect coda to the sailing scene — Dan's uncomprehending 'What's the fucking difference?' collapses the entire pompous sailing hierarchy. Works as a straight man's puncture of the absurdity, and it's pure Dan: aggressive, oblivious, economical.

jokes.text: Is this the amount of times you've admired yourself in the mirror today? No, I'm guessing that's the difference in salary between this job and the one that you just interviewed for.
jokes.setup: Amy is muttering numbers to herself at her desk
jokes.punchline: Dan pivots from the snarky mirror quip to revealing he already knows about Amy's secret job interview
jokes.explanation: Solid setup-punchline with character work — the misdirection from vanity to workplace scheming is well-structured. Dan's predatory intelligence is well-served here.

jokes.text: Simple makeup, higher neckline. Flats don't go with that dress, which means you probably have heels in your bag. Coffee from corner bakery implying you were at one of the lobbying shops on 18th.
jokes.setup: Amy asks how Dan could possibly know about her interview
jokes.punchline: Dan produces a Sherlock Holmes-style deduction chain that's both impressive and creepy — all from superficial observations
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed Sherlock parody that simultaneously reveals Dan's surveillance-level obsession with office politics. The specificity of the clues ('corner bakery' → lobbying shop) is funny precisely because it's disproportionate effort for petty workplace intelligence-gathering. Good structure in the escalating chain.

jokes.text: I think he'll be pulling Bon Jovi out of a burning tank in case we all forgot what a fucking war hero he is.
jokes.setup: Dan's asked if Danny Chung will be performing at the dinner
jokes.punchline: The sarcastic suggestion that Chung needs to theatrically re-establish his war hero credentials, combined with the absurd image of 'pulling Bon Jovi out of a burning tank'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Bon Jovi' as the rescued celebrity is the magic ingredient — it's not just any celebrity, it's the most flag-waving, middle-America, military-friendly rock star imaginable. The joke punctures manufactured heroism with satirical precision. Quotable.

jokes.text: It looks like I'm tweeting when a guy loses his leg.
jokes.setup: The wrong photo has been released — one showing Selina apparently on her phone during the hostage crisis
jokes.punchline: Selina's brutally self-aware summation is the most damning possible interpretation of the image
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The specificity and economy are exceptional — 'tweeting when a guy loses his leg' packages political image disaster, social media anxiety, and war into seven words. Highly quotable, immediately understandable, and perfectly in character for Selina's self-aware catastrophizing. Earns 8.0 craft — it's not legendary but it's genuinely impressive writing. The 'loses his leg' detail is darkly specific in a way that elevates it above generic 'phone at wrong time' jokes.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'm stabbing him. I'm going to go stab him.' / Amy reacts deadpan.
jokes.setup: Selina believes Kent released the bad photo to undermine her.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate, matter-of-fact decision to commit literal stabbing — stated twice, with increasing commitment — and Amy's complete non-reaction treats it as a routine management decision.
jokes.explanation: A reliable Veep mode — hyperbolic threat delivered deadpan. Functional but not among the episode's best. The repetition ('I'm stabbing him. I'm going to go stab him.') is a minor escalation beat.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I'll start running the acid bath.' — offering to destroy evidence.
jokes.setup: Selina kicks everyone out, saying she doesn't want witnesses.
jokes.punchline: Gary, ever the devoted aide, immediately offers to run an acid bath — treating witness disposal as just another task on his to-do list.
jokes.explanation: Gary's total devotion expressed in the most extreme terms is a running character note. 'Start running the acid bath' works because he says it with the same energy as 'I'll get the car.' High character integration.

jokes.text: Vis-à-vis jowls. / That's how he looks. That's his face.
jokes.setup: Kent says the other photos caught POTUS at a 'bad angle'
jokes.punchline: Selina punctures the euphemism — that's not a bad angle, that's just the president's actual face
jokes.explanation: Good callback and deadpan puncture of political euphemism. The Latin 'vis-à-vis' from Kent followed by Selina's blunt takedown is a nice rhythm. 'That's his face' is the whole joke in three words.

jokes.text: Why didn't you just take the good one of me, Photoshop it in? / I can't airbrush history, ma'am. I'm not Joseph Stalin.
jokes.setup: Selina asks why they can't just digitally alter the photo to use her good image
jokes.punchline: Kent invokes Stalin — the man literally famous for airbrushing people out of history — as a moral standard he won't sink to
jokes.explanation: A very strong joke. The irony is perfect — Stalin's exact historical legacy is photographic falsification, so invoking him as a principled standard of authenticity is a precise reversal. The follow-up ('You're not. You don't have a tenth of his charm') lands the second punch. Both halves work. Earns 8.0 craft: the historical reference is deployed with precision and the two-beat structure is clean.

jokes.text: No, that's true. You're not. You don't have a tenth of his charm.
jokes.setup: Kent says he's not Joseph Stalin
jokes.punchline: Selina agrees — but the agreement is a deeper insult, casting Kent as less charismatic than a genocidal dictator
jokes.explanation: The episode's best single line. Selina's agreement with the Stalin comparison, redirected into an insult about charm, is exactly the kind of dark, absurdist wit Veep does at its peak. Calling Stalin more charming than Kent is funny because it's almost plausible given Kent's affect. It's perfectly economical, deeply in character for Selina's cutting wit, and highly quotable. Earns 8.3 craft — this is genuinely exceptional line writing.

jokes.text: Oh, so now I have to have Dan and Mike, Rodgers and Hammershit, come up with a new song in an afternoon?
jokes.setup: Selina is told the Speaker song is cut and she needs new material
jokes.punchline: 'Hammershit' as a portmanteau insult replacing 'Hammerstein' — dismissing Dan and Mike's creative abilities
jokes.explanation: Selina's insult portmanteaus are a show specialty. 'Hammershit' for Hammerstein is clean, mean, and perfectly calibrated — it replaces the suffix with maximum contempt while keeping the form recognizable. Good Veep-style wordplay.

jokes.text: She was multitasking. It's a thing women can do, like smelling nice and wrapping gifts.
jokes.setup: They're trying to spin the photo of Selina appearing to be on her phone during the crisis
jokes.punchline: The suggested defense is a condescendingly sexist framing that reduces multitasking to 'smelling nice and wrapping gifts'
jokes.explanation: The joke is the oblivious sexism of the suggested spin — 'smelling nice and wrapping gifts' as the two examples of female multitasking is a well-chosen absurdity. Somewhat standard Veep male-incompetence territory but the specific examples elevate it.

jokes.text: What are you laughing about, Jolly Green Jizzface?
jokes.setup: Selina is stressed about the photo situation; Jonah is smirking in the background
jokes.punchline: 'Jolly Green Jizzface' — a Veep-trademarked compound insult targeting Jonah's height, coloring, and generally objectionable nature
jokes.explanation: Peak Veep insult comedy. 'Jolly Green Jizzface' is a perfect specimen of the show's compound-noun profane nickname formula for Jonah. It references his height (Jolly Green Giant) while being maximally degrading. Entirely character-specific — only Selina roasts Jonah this way, and only Jonah could inspire this nickname. Highly quotable. Earns the 8.0 craft score for originality, character integration, and economy of the insult's construction.

jokes.text: Oh, I came here to tell you that you're a meme, ma'am. I'm a meme ma'am? What are you talking about? Speak English, boy.
jokes.setup: Jonah says Selina is 'a meme, ma'am'
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears/misunderstands 'meme ma'am' as a single phrase — 'meme ma'am' — and the homophonic confusion compounds into a lovely verbal tangle
jokes.explanation: A well-engineered wordplay/confusion joke. 'Meme, ma'am' sounds like 'meme ma'am' and Selina's confusion ('I'm a meme ma'am?') reflects her generation gap with internet culture. The 'Speak English, boy' is good Selina condescension. 'Not a meme ma'am, just a meme, ma'am' is a lovely clarification that extends the joke.

jokes.text: You at the Declaration of Independence... With Mary Magdalene at the crucifixion, the 2004 tsunami.
jokes.setup: Jonah describes the Photoshopped memes placing Selina's phone-distracted image into historical moments
jokes.punchline: The escalating series of contexts — Declaration of Independence to crucifixion to tsunami — each more inappropriate than the last
jokes.explanation: The escalation from founding history to religious suffering to natural disaster is well-calibrated. The 2004 tsunami as the endpoint is a good dark choice — it's recent enough to feel transgressive. The setup is efficiently delivered through a list.

jokes.text: If there was a tsunami, you'd be genuinely looking at your phone 'cause you'd be checking the weather.
jokes.setup: Jonah says Selina has been Photoshopped into the 2004 tsunami
jokes.punchline: Selina defends herself by pointing out that if there were a real tsunami, she would legitimately be on her phone — which is not actually a defense
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy — Selina's 'defense' accidentally confirms the criticism. She's phone-obsessed, and her instinct to defend herself actually proves the point. The 'checking the weather' rationalization is pitch-perfect: it's both plausible and completely beside the point. Strong character integration and structure. Earns the near-8 craft score.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, yeah. Okay, yeah, that's a cherry on top of this whole turd cake.
jokes.setup: Selina learns Jonah will be at the dinner that's already going badly
jokes.punchline: 'Cherry on top of this whole turd cake' — an anatomically grotesque inversion of the standard idiom
jokes.explanation: Another strong Selina-specific insult coinage. 'Turd cake' as the inverted 'icing on the cake' idiom is economical and disgusting in exactly the right proportion. Very quotable. The phrase feels immediately usable in real life.

jokes.text: Ooh, your leg. Could you put...? Fix your leg so I can see your whole leg. Fix it. Yeah. Okay.
jokes.setup: Selina is stressed and distracted, and Gary is nearby
jokes.punchline: Selina's objectifying, oddly clinical instruction to Gary about his leg posture — 'fix your leg' repeated as if Gary is furniture being rearranged
jokes.explanation: A visual cringe moment that works entirely through Selina's relationship with Gary — she treats him like a piece of furniture/assistant to the point of literally repositioning his limbs. The blankness of 'fix it' and 'okay' as she satisfies herself is quietly appalling and very funny on reflection.

jokes.text: You're out of your head, dude. That would have made people piss in their pants.
jokes.setup: Selina says the cut Speaker song was 'not that hilarious'
jokes.punchline: Dan defends it with the highest possible praise: it would have made people urinate involuntarily
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Dan's disproportionate investment in the comedy song is mildly funny but the joke itself ('piss in their pants' as the benchmark) is standard hyperbole without much craft.

jokes.text: Three words... Weird Al Yankovic.
jokes.setup: Mike says comedy songs are 'the mark of a douchebag'
jokes.punchline: Dan's definitive rebuttal — 'Three words: Weird Al Yankovic' — is already undermined by being four words
jokes.explanation: A neatly constructed joke — the 'three words' setup that's immediately violated by Weird Al's four-syllable name is a classic structural irony. Dan defending parody songs with Weird Al as his pinnacle of achievement is also characterful.

jokes.text: Just protect the endangered goose, Bruce. / These are all great. / If the first one bombs, people are gonna be sitting out there thinking, 'Shit the bed, we got 49 more of these to go.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Mike workshop the '50 Ways to Leave Your Lover' parody, with Dan praising the rhyme scheme
jokes.punchline: Mike's assessment that if the first verse bombs, the audience will dread the remaining 49 — the structure of the song becomes the trap
jokes.explanation: A meta-joke about the song format they've chosen that also works as real comedy theory — the structural problem of a 50-part song is genuinely acute and Mike's panicked articulation of it is funny. 'Shit the bed, we got 49 more of these to go' is a good image.

jokes.text: Nobody can [get in with Kent]. He's half robot, half robot.
jokes.setup: Dan is frustrated he can't get in with Kent
jokes.punchline: 'Half robot, half robot' — the tautology emphasizes that Kent is 100% robot with zero human component
jokes.explanation: An extremely well-constructed tautological joke. The standard idiom would be 'half robot, half something human' — Mike's version eliminates the human half entirely by repeating 'robot,' which is funnier than any human/robot contrast could be. Perfect economy. Justifies 8+ impact: the phrase is quotable and the logic is airtight.

jokes.text: In college all the girls liked me. There was this one girl, smart, who had no interest in me. She wasn't gay either. I checked.
jokes.setup: Dan explains his obsession with winning over Kent as analogous to a college situation
jokes.punchline: 'She wasn't gay either. I checked.' — the parenthetical investigation into the girl's sexuality, delivered as due diligence
jokes.explanation: Strong character joke — 'I checked' implies Dan actually investigated the question, which is both creepy and revealing. The framing of eliminating variables as an excuse for his failure is very Dan. The setup ('all the girls liked me') primes the reversal well.

jokes.text: And did you? / No. / And that failure has haunted me to this very day.
jokes.setup: Dan tells the story of the one girl who rejected him and frames it as a mission
jokes.punchline: He failed. And it haunts him. The anticlimactic admission of failure paired with 'haunted me to this very day' is mockumentary confessional gold
jokes.explanation: The structural setup is excellent — the big mission speech primes us for a triumph, and the flat 'No' is perfectly deflating. 'Haunted me to this very day' is overdramatic enough to be funny. Good rhythm.

jokes.text: I have a similar story, but the other way around. In college there was only one girl that was interested in me. And it turns out she was actually a lesbian.
jokes.setup: Dan's sad story about the one girl who rejected him
jokes.punchline: Mike's inverse — he was only interesting to one girl ever, and she turned out to be a lesbian — the symmetry and the bleakness are both the punchline
jokes.explanation: The perfect structural callback and inversion. Where Dan's story is about the one woman who resisted him in a sea of admirers, Mike's is about being interesting to exactly one woman — who wasn't even attracted to men. The parallel structure, the beat before the punchline, and the deep self-deprecation make this one of the episode's better comedic exchanges. The pause before 'she was actually a lesbian' lands it.

jokes.text: You are incredibly valued here and I was wondering if there's anything we could do to make you want to stay with us. / More money, ma'am. / I'm on it. Welcome back. / Honored to serve.
jokes.setup: Selina makes an elaborate diplomatic speech about how valued Sue is
jokes.punchline: Sue cuts straight to 'more money' and the entire retention drama is resolved in four seconds
jokes.explanation: Sue's economy of expression is a running joke in Veep, and this might be its purest expression. Selina's whole politically managed retention speech is obliterated in two words. 'Honored to serve' is Sue immediately reverting to her usual toneless formality. Beautifully structured micro-scene.

jokes.text: Just bought Sue for a dollar. Who's next?
jokes.setup: Selina has just secured Sue's loyalty with a pay raise
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes the entire interaction as a transaction — 'bought Sue for a dollar' — with gleeful cynicism
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character voice — Selina's transactional view of loyalty is compressed into six words. 'Bought Sue for a dollar' is a great phrase. The 'Who's next?' as if shopping at a market is a good extension of the metaphor.

jokes.text: I've never eaten with Gary before. / I've eaten next to him, but...
jokes.setup: Amy suggests Selina invite Gary to dinner to patch things up
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual admission that she has never actually eaten WITH Gary — only adjacent to him — captures their non-relationship with absurd precision
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a standout moment in the whole series for the Selina/Gary dynamic. The distinction between 'eaten with' and 'eaten next to' as a complete characterization of their relationship — Gary is furniture, not a companion — is both funny and quietly devastating. Excellent economy, exceptional character integration. The trailing 'but...' implies a universe of unstated dehumanization. Earns the 8.5 craft score.

jokes.text: Good morning, Jonad. / You're pathetic. / Jonuts!
jokes.setup: Jonah has been given West Exec parking and is enjoying his status
jokes.punchline: Dan and others taunt Jonah with nicknames as he passes — 'Jonad' and 'Jonuts' as abbreviated insults
jokes.explanation: Running nickname game with Jonah is fun but these particular variants are less inspired than the episode's better Jonah insults. They work as rhythmic texture.

jokes.text: Everyone hates him. / Yeah, but we hated him first. / Oh, yeah, we hated him long before it was even fashionable to hate him.
jokes.setup: Dan and Mike observe that everyone in the building hates Jonah
jokes.punchline: They take proprietary pride in having hated Jonah before it was trendy — framing hatred of a colleague as a hipster credential
jokes.explanation: A well-crafted joke applying hipster credentialism to institutional hatred. 'Before it was fashionable to hate him' is a very quotable construction. The absurdity of competitive early-adoption of contempt is the engine.

jokes.text: This is like a wildlife documentary. Jonah in his natural habitat. He might defecate in a bush.
jokes.setup: They're watching Jonah walk through the building after being given West Exec parking
jokes.punchline: Mike frames Jonah as a large, uncivilized animal being observed — the 'might defecate in a bush' specificity is the payoff
jokes.explanation: Mike's deadpan nature documentary commentary on Jonah is a good bit — 'defecate in a bush' is specific enough to be funny. The observational framing is well-executed.

jokes.text: Yeah, he's just feeling bad. He's... you know, he's sugary. Or he's not sugary enough.
jokes.setup: Dan and Mike press Jonah about what the symptoms of Type 2 diabetes are
jokes.punchline: Jonah clearly doesn't know anything about diabetes and reduces it to 'he's sugary. Or he's not sugary enough' — the vague binary is the joke
jokes.explanation: A quietly brilliant piece of Jonah stupidity. 'He's sugary. Or he's not sugary enough' as a medical explanation for diabetes is perfect — it's technically related to glucose but rendered in the most childish possible framing. The uncertainty ('or he's not sugary enough') doubles down. Very quotable.

jokes.text: You're obsolete. You're like an old VCR, but with a bigger mouth.
jokes.setup: Dan and Mike confront Jonah about being frozen out by Kent
jokes.punchline: 'Old VCR but with a bigger mouth' — the obsolescence metaphor combined with the physical insult targeting Jonah's large features
jokes.explanation: Good Veep insult — combining obsolescence with the physical dig about Jonah's appearance (his large mouth is an established physical characteristic) is efficient. 'VCR' is a well-chosen period-specific technology.

jokes.text: You've been following me like Nancy Drew and his butt-sniffing dog, and I'm the one that has nothing to do?
jokes.setup: Jonah learns Dan and Mike have been following him around the building
jokes.punchline: The gender flip on Nancy Drew ('his butt-sniffing dog'), combined with Jonah turning their mockery back on them for having nothing better to do than surveil him
jokes.explanation: 'His butt-sniffing dog' is a good modifier — Nancy Drew's dog is a girl's companion reimagined as a crude male animal. The logical reversal (Jonah pointing out that watching him aimlessly is more pointless than his own aimlessness) is a good comeback for a character who rarely wins exchanges.

jokes.text: Well, I might have nothing to do, gentlemen, but at least when I'm crying about it, I am crying about it in my car in West Wing Exec parking.
jokes.setup: Dan and Mike have been mocking Jonah for having nothing to do
jokes.punchline: Jonah concedes the point entirely — yes, he's crying in his car — but the West Exec parking elevates it. The callback to the episode's opening parking gag lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two or three best jokes and a candidate for the standout moment. The structure is exceptional: Jonah fully concedes he is crying alone in a car, which is pathetic — but the West Exec parking status redeems it on his own terms. The callback to the episode's cold open makes it a perfect structural payoff. The willingness to admit 'I am crying about it' while still holding the status card is both deeply in character and genuinely funny. 'West Wing Exec parking' as the ultimate consolation prize for a hollow status-obsessed man-child is the episode in miniature. Earns the 9.0+ scores.

jokes.text: ♪ No, no, no, don't sing it with me / That fucks me up. ♪
jokes.setup: Selina is warming up her voice for the dinner performance by singing scales
jokes.punchline: Gary joins in, Selina snaps at him — even in the act of helping her, Gary's participation is unwelcome
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina/Gary relationship texture. The profanity in a music context ('that fucks me up') is well-placed but the joke is minor.

jokes.text: Now, am I getting older or are you getting younger? / We're both getting older. / That's the third option, of course. That's great stuff.
jokes.setup: The Speaker makes a flirtatious pseudo-compliment to Amy
jokes.punchline: Amy takes the rhetorical question literally and gives the logical answer — they're both aging. The Speaker's delighted 'third option' is the comedy of his flattery being literally demolished
jokes.explanation: Amy's brutal literalism dismantling a political pleasantry is excellent character comedy. The Speaker's gracious recovery ('third option') is equally good — he laughs at himself for being caught. Well-structured two-beat exchange.

jokes.text: There will be no jokes from me. I'm not going to mention the photograph. [beat] Were you Internet shopping?
jokes.setup: The Speaker formally declares a joke truce — no jokes about the photo
jokes.punchline: He immediately breaks the truce with the most obvious possible joke about the photo ('Were you Internet shopping?'), then clarifies 'I'm joking. Or rather, I'm not.'
jokes.explanation: The joke-truce-immediately-violated structure is a classic, but the 'I'm joking. Or rather, I'm not.' is a very good clarification — it reveals the barb was real. The beat before the internet shopping joke is well-placed.

jokes.text: They seemed very excited about the possibility of a veteran becoming president. Not necessarily me. / Well, they seemed very excited about the possibility of a woman becoming president. Not necessarily you.
jokes.setup: Marwood and Selina engage in superficially friendly conversation about the prayer breakfast
jokes.punchline: They perfectly mirror each other's backhanded qualifications — each undermining the other's presidential aspirations while maintaining the form of a compliment
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed political double-insult. The perfect structural mirroring — 'not necessarily me' reflected back as 'not necessarily you' — is the joke's engine. Both characters maintain the form of pleasantry while landing clean rhetorical gut punches. The structure is arguably a 9.0 — it's formally perfect. Earns high scores across the board.

jokes.text: Hey, it is good to be on top of international relations. So there are fewer opportunities for guys like me to risk our lives. And limbs.
jokes.setup: Selina boasts about her enhanced foreign policy role
jokes.punchline: Marwood implies her diplomatic work reduces the need for soldiers to lose limbs — an indirect compliment that emphasizes his own sacrifice
jokes.explanation: The 'And limbs' addendum is the punchline — a quiet escalation that lands the real point (Marwood is a veteran amputee) without melodrama. The deadpan addition of 'And limbs' as a throwaway is well-executed.

jokes.text: Amy, you should stop eating so much. / What the fuck are you talking about? / You're stress eating. / I'm not. I'm having dessert.
jokes.setup: Dan volunteers unsolicited commentary on Amy's eating
jokes.punchline: Amy's indignant denial ('I'm not. I'm having dessert') is funny because the distinction she's drawing is not actually a distinction
jokes.explanation: Good cringe exchange — Dan's oblivious intrusion into Amy's eating is character-true, and Amy's response that she's 'having dessert' (not stress eating) is self-deceiving in a funny way.

jokes.text: Find yourself a political nerd who also showers. I'm just saying I'm looking out for you. I wouldn't want you to become a fat, neurotic freak. Speaking as a friend.
jokes.setup: Dan tells Amy she needs to date and takes care of herself
jokes.punchline: 'I wouldn't want you to become a fat, neurotic freak. Speaking as a friend.' — the 'Speaking as a friend' tacked onto an insult that no friend would deliver
jokes.explanation: Dan's performance of friendship while delivering what is clearly not friendship is classic. 'Speaking as a friend' as a tag on 'fat neurotic freak' is the essence of his character. 'A political nerd who also showers' is a good specific qualifier — very Washington DC.

jokes.text: You saved four guys from captivity. Yeah, and they weren't burnt, by the way. Chung's guy had a face like a Christmas ham.
jokes.setup: Selina and Marwood compare his war heroism to Chung's
jokes.punchline: 'Chung's guy had a face like a Christmas ham' — brutally specific description of burns as a festive meat product
jokes.explanation: Genuinely exceptional dark joke. 'A face like a Christmas ham' for burn scarring is both horrible and exact — it's specific enough to be a real image (the glazed, red-brown surface of a Christmas ham as a description of burn scars) and the festive context makes it worse. Highly quotable and genuinely original in its cruelty. Earns 8.0 craft — the originality score (8.5) reflects a genuinely unusual comic image.

jokes.text: Danny Chung beatboxing at the political dinner — the crowd going wild while Selina's team stares dead-eyed.
jokes.setup: Governor Chung announces a 'beatbox challenge' at a formal political dinner.
jokes.punchline: The crowd erupts with cheering while the transcript reveals Selina and her team's dead-eyed contempt for the performance.
jokes.explanation: The visual/dramatic irony of the crowd's enthusiasm vs. Selina's team's contempt is the joke. The political theater of Chung's performative populism landing perfectly is bitter for Selina to watch. Inferred from context — 'That isn't impressive at all. He's just spitting, right?'

jokes.text: That isn't impressive at all. He's just spitting, right?
jokes.setup: Governor Chung is beatboxing to appreciative applause
jokes.punchline: Selina watching her political rival get applause for beatboxing and dismissing it as 'just spitting'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — Selina's dismissive jealousy of Chung's populism through deflation. 'He's just spitting, right?' is a funny mischaracterization born of resentment.

jokes.text: Of course, all she had to do was unlock her bedroom door, but thanks anyway.
jokes.setup: Chung has just thanked Selina for her role in freeing the hostages
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts his own praise with a sexual innuendo suggesting the 'hostages' were in her bedroom — turning political praise into a sexual jab
jokes.explanation: A precise political roast joke — the setup (genuine praise) makes the punchline (sexual dismissal) hit harder. The structure of apparently complimenting then undercutting is well-executed. 'Unlock her bedroom door' is an efficient innuendo.

jokes.text: Well, at least she didn't look down her nose at 'em. You know, when they're looking for the thing.
jokes.setup: Following Chung's bedroom door quip at Selina's expense
jokes.punchline: Marwood piles on with a visual sex joke — Selina 'looking down her nose' in a sexual context — though it's deliberately obscure enough to deniable
jokes.explanation: Weaker joke — deliberately obscure, which means it's not quite clear enough to land cleanly. The vagueness ('the thing') undermines it.

jokes.text: I need a joke. I need a joke.
jokes.setup: Selina is being roasted by political opponents on stage and is visibly rattled
jokes.punchline: The whispered plea 'I need a joke, I need a joke' — the desperation of a politician caught without a comeback is the whole joke
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat — the show of vulnerability in a public political performance setting is character-appropriate and funny in the moment.

jokes.text: I'll tell you, that's one speaker I'd like to put on mute.
jokes.setup: The emcee has just introduced Speaker Marwood by name
jokes.punchline: Selina's pun on 'Speaker' (his title) / 'speaker' (an audio device) — she'd like to mute him
jokes.explanation: A solid pun — it's the kind of joke that works in the room because of context and timing. 'Speaker on mute' is a well-constructed if slightly obvious play on words. Not inspired but cleanly executed.

jokes.text: You just need to keep in mind that I'm not Selina Dion.
jokes.setup: Selina is about to perform her comedy song
jokes.punchline: 'Selina Dion' — her name substituted for Celine Dion, pre-emptively lowering expectations for her singing
jokes.explanation: A clean name-substitution pun made special by the character's name being Selina, making it feel earned rather than random. Good character integration because the joke requires Selina's specific first name. Very quotable and the show knows it — it's a deliberate showcase line.

jokes.text: ♪ Kiss a fat baby, Abie / Set yourself free ♪
jokes.setup: The song gives advice for winning elections Paul Simon-style
jokes.punchline: 'Kiss a fat baby, Abie' — the political 'kiss babies' cliché rendered with the bluntness of 'fat baby' and rhymed against the song structure
jokes.explanation: The most memorable lyric in the song — 'fat baby' lands as the specific, slightly cruel word choice that elevates it above generic political satire. The name 'Abie' as the rhyme partner is a good absurdist touch.

jokes.text: This is imbecilic. / You're right. It's asinine. / No, keep smiling, Mr. Ryan. / I'm smiling falsely. So should you.
jokes.setup: Kent and Dan watch the song performance from the audience
jokes.punchline: Kent's deadpan instructions to maintain a false smile while mutually acknowledging the song is 'imbecilic' is a perfect distillation of political performance
jokes.explanation: A standout moment. The exchange is perfectly structured: Kent says 'imbecilic,' Dan agrees 'asinine,' Kent corrects course with 'keep smiling,' Dan acknowledges 'I'm smiling falsely.' The whole exchange is about maintaining performance while having a private conversation — and both characters are equally guilty of it. Exceptional character integration for Kent especially. 'I'm smiling falsely. So should you' is excellent deadpan. Earns 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: You killed it. / You killed better than Chung killed, and he's actually killed.
jokes.setup: Amy congratulates Selina on the song's success
jokes.punchline: 'You killed better than Chung killed, and he's actually killed' — the slang term 'killed it' (succeeded) vs. literal wartime killing, landing on Chung's veteran status
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best line and a candidate for one of the best jokes in the series. The double meaning of 'killed' (performance slang vs. literal combat killing) is deployed with perfect economy and maximum shock. The pivot from entertainment compliment to war reality in a single sentence is exactly what Veep does at its absolute peak. It's dark, it's fast, it requires no explanation, and it says something real about the political world where veterans and performers compete in the same arena. Comparable in quality to the 'Snip, snap' anchor — 9.0 structure, 9.5 economy, 10.0 quotability. This is what an exceptional Veep line looks like.

jokes.text: I had to pretend not to like it to Kent while pretending to like it like he was pretending to like it, but he didn't actually like it and I actually really liked it.
jokes.setup: Selina debrefs the performance for her team
jokes.punchline: The recursive pretending — pretending not to like it while pretending to like it while Kent pretended to like it but didn't — is the Veep political performance paradox in verbal form
jokes.explanation: A characteristically Veep piece of verbal recursion. The layer upon layer of performed vs. genuine response is genuinely witty and also a pointed political observation about how these people perform for each other constantly. Slightly less economical than the best jokes but structurally impressive.

jokes.text: Kent to Selina: 'Ma'am, your song last night made my lips curl.' (pause) 'Upwards.'
jokes.setup: Kent approaches Selina the morning after the dinner.
jokes.punchline: Kent's compliment — 'made my lips curl' — sounds like a complaint until the pause and 'Upwards.' The pause is essential; without it, 'lips curl' reads as disgust.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. Kent expressing a smile as 'my lips curl... upwards' is a perfect encapsulation of his character — he is so emotionally removed from human expression that he describes smiling as a clinical direction of lip movement, with the direction specified as clarification. The pause is everything. This is exactly what the 8.5 calibration anchor looks like, and it exceeds it. Maximally quotable, deeply character-specific, structurally perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, you want Dan? / No, I want Mike.
jokes.setup: Kent says he wants to borrow a team member
jokes.punchline: Selina assumes Kent wants Dan (the ambitious schemer who has been pursuing Kent all episode) — Kent wants Mike
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the episode's Dan-pursuing-Kent subplot. Dan's entire arc of trying to impress Kent culminates in Kent not even wanting him. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: Uh, no, you don't want me, sir. You do want Dan. He's great.
jokes.setup: Kent says he wants Mike, not Dan
jokes.punchline: Mike desperately deflects to Dan — the opposite of what almost anyone in Washington would do
jokes.explanation: Mike's aversion to ambition and responsibility is perfectly rendered here — he actively tries to give the prestigious assignment to someone else. The 'He's great' is a good anxious add-on.

jokes.text: Selina being offered Helsinki in exchange for Mike — her dawning horror and eventual capitulation.
jokes.setup: Kent has just asked to borrow Mike. Selina refuses. Then Kent reveals POTUS can't make the Helsinki agreement.
jokes.punchline: Selina is forced to accept Mike's reassignment AND go to Helsinki — the price for keeping Helsinki is losing Mike for two weeks. Her 'Dream Metric thing sounds kind of fantastic' is the humiliated reversal.
jokes.explanation: The political chess move is satisfying: Selina tries to hold a line and loses everything. Her pivot — 'Dream Metric thing sounds kind of fantastic' — is a forced enthusiasm that's painful and funny. Escalation structure works well.

jokes.text: Mike, on second thought, I think that this whole Dream... / Metric. / Dream Metric thing sounds kind of fantastic.
jokes.setup: Moments ago Selina was refusing to give up Mike — then she learns she's being sent to Europe
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant reversal — she now enthusiastically endorses Dream Metric (and losing Mike) now that the Europe trip is better than the alternative
jokes.explanation: The reversal on 'Dream Metric sounds fantastic' combined with Selina not remembering the name ('Metric' prompted by Kent) is a good two-beat joke. The political volatility of her positions is on full display.

jokes.text: Ma'am, please don't let him take me. I don't want to go to numbers camp. / I can't help you. Just let go.
jokes.setup: Kent has claimed Mike for Dream Metric and Mike physically clings to Selina
jokes.punchline: 'Numbers camp' as Mike's term for Dream Metric — the reduction of a prestigious White House analytics program to something you'd dread as a child
jokes.explanation: 'Numbers camp' is a great phrase — the childlike horror of being sent away for remedial math combined with the physical clinging makes this a strong comedic moment. Mike's character (incompetent, math-averse, job-avoiding) is perfectly served by this image. High quotability.

jokes.text: I don't make impressions, ma'am. I've literally lived with people who insist I was never there.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Mike he must have made an impression on Kent
jokes.punchline: Mike's claim that he makes no impression — evidenced by people he lived with 'insisting he was never there' — is both self-deprecating and likely true
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. 'I've literally lived with people who insist I was never there' is a devastating and very specific line — it implies a whole personal history of un-noticeability. It's also funny because it's clearly delivered with total sincerity rather than self-pity. The concept of cohabitants denying your existence is original and very Mike. One of the episode's standout character lines. Earns 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: You give no press briefings. Understood? / I become Mike, you become an ugly me.
jokes.setup: Selina instructs Dan on how to serve as an acting communications director
jokes.punchline: 'You become an ugly me' — Selina explains her communications role as essentially being herself, but Dan would be doing it worse
jokes.explanation: 'An ugly me' is a good Selina construction — it's an insult to Dan and a compliment to herself simultaneously. Clean delivery.

jokes.text: Ma'am, I'm gonna get you major online traction. Reddit, Tumblr, boom.
jokes.setup: Dan takes on new media responsibilities
jokes.punchline: Dan's confidence about Reddit and Tumblr is deeply ironic given that Jonah's Reddit/Tumblr posting is what causes the episode's climactic crisis
jokes.explanation: Works as dramatic irony for viewers paying attention — the Reddit/Tumblr confidence is about to be spectacularly undermined by Jonah's unauthorized posting. Good structural setup for the coming crisis.

jokes.text: Whatever. Just don't use my bathroom on Air Force Two. / There's another bathroom?
jokes.setup: Selina warns Dan not to use her bathroom on Air Force Two
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'There's another bathroom?' reveals he would happily use her private bathroom if he'd known it existed
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional joke about Dan's sense of entitlement. The rapid exchange is cleanly built.

jokes.text: So cut to I'd set up a business importing pecorino cheese. Are you familiar with that brand of cheese? / Uh, I think... / It's like parmesan, but it's different. / They're very different.
jokes.setup: Dana is telling a story at what is supposed to be a special intimate dinner for Gary
jokes.punchline: Dana calls pecorino a 'brand' (it's a type) — Selina makes a corrective noise, Dana doubles down with 'it's like parmesan, but it's different' and Selina tersely agrees 'they're very different'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy — Dana's enthusiastic incompetence with cheese combined with Selina's barely suppressed contempt is well-played. The specificity of the pecorino/parmesan distinction being wrong in multiple directions is a nice detail.

jokes.text: And they would have made her an offer that she couldn't refuse. / Actually, the mafia involvement in the dairy industry is minimal. / You're right. I'm sorry, I didn't mean to be flippant. / Think about it. In 'The Godfather,' no cheese.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a Godfather joke about the Sicilian dairy connection
jokes.punchline: Dana corrects the Godfather joke with 'mafia involvement in the dairy industry is minimal' — a straight-faced refutation of a joke that required no refutation, followed by the escalation 'In The Godfather, no cheese'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a standout Dana moment. The refutation of a clearly jokey Godfather reference on factual grounds is excellent cringe comedy. Then 'Think about it. In The Godfather, no cheese' is an inspired doubling-down — Dana is so committed to the dairy/mafia point that she's conducted what appears to be a Godfather review specifically for cheese. 'No cheese' as the cinematic observation is absurd, specific, and funny. Earns 8.4 craft — the concept is highly original and the structure of the exchange is tight.

jokes.text: Dana correcting Selina on pecorino vs. parmesan: 'Pecorino.' / '...the pecorino.' / 'I'm sorry.'
jokes.setup: Selina keeps calling the cheese parmesan despite being corrected.
jokes.punchline: Selina's inability to retain 'pecorino' — or her passive resistance to caring — is a small recurring beat in the lunch scene.
jokes.explanation: A minor running gag within the scene. Gets a smile but not a laugh. Functions as texture for the cringe escalation.

jokes.text: Boom! That's her catchphrase. She got you. You got to be so careful.
jokes.setup: Dana delivers a 'boom!' punchline during her cheese anecdote
jokes.punchline: Gary's delighted framing of Dana's 'boom' as a catchphrase — and his warning that 'you got to be so careful' around her
jokes.explanation: Gary's completely disproportionate admiration for Dana's barely impressive cheese anecdote is a good character beat. 'You got to be so careful' implies Dana is a formidable rhetorical opponent when she clearly isn't.

jokes.text: A new Leviathan! / A Lev... a new Leviathan! / Isn't that great?
jokes.setup: Selina gives Gary a bag as a gift
jokes.punchline: The brand name 'Leviathan' (clearly a high-end bag) delivered with Selina's pride and Gary's barely contained ecstasy is the joke
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to Gary's bag obsession and the intimacy of Selina actually engaging with his interests. Warm cringe comedy. The bag obsession running gag pays off here naturally.

jokes.text: Does it have the same layout, honey? Because it took you so long to learn the other one. / No, it's fine. I can learn a new layout. It's fine.
jokes.setup: Gary receives a new designer bag from Selina
jokes.punchline: Dana's apparently supportive question about layout ('it took you so long to learn the other one') is a backhanded remark that infantilizes Gary in front of his boss
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment — Dana accidentally (or knowingly) humiliates Gary in front of Selina by implying he took a long time to learn how to use a handbag. Gary's defensive 'It's fine' reinforces his subordinate position simultaneously with his boss and his girlfriend.

jokes.text: ♪ I love my new bag! ♪ / I can just sew some new pockets in. That's okay.
jokes.setup: Gary is over the moon about the new bag
jokes.punchline: He sings about it, then immediately begins problem-solving the bag's inadequacies — the gift isn't perfect but he'll make it work
jokes.explanation: The spontaneous singing followed by the immediate adjustment ('I can just sew some new pockets in') is peak Gary. The song is pure joy; the 'sew new pockets' is the sad reality that this special gift has practical limitations he'll need to work around. Character integration is a 9.0.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I can just sew some new pockets in. That's okay.'
jokes.setup: Gary has just expressed joy about the bag but there's an apparent issue with the layout.
jokes.punchline: Gary's instant rationalization — he'll just sew pockets in, no problem — is the response of someone who will find a way to love anything Selina gives him, layout flaw and all.
jokes.explanation: A small but perfectly in-character Gary beat. His immediate accommodation of the flaw — 'I can just sew some new pockets' — is both sad and sweet. His devotion as a coping mechanism.

jokes.text: I won't touch anything. Oops, I just started a war with Iran. / Joke. / That's not even remotely funny. / That's just one of my classic jokes.
jokes.setup: Dana grabs Selina's iPad and promises not to do anything with it
jokes.punchline: 'Oops, I just started a war with Iran' — the VP's iPad can do that; the casual delivery; and then the meta-defence 'that's just one of my classic jokes'
jokes.explanation: A very good dark joke — the casual nuclear-level accident framing ('Oops, I just started a war with Iran') is funny because of its plausibility in this context. Dana's follow-up 'That's just one of my classic jokes' is a great meta-joke about terrible joke-tellers who self-label their material.

jokes.text: Our European visit just got turned into a one-way trip to hell and back. / That's actually a return.
jokes.setup: Selina says the European visit has become 'a one-way trip to hell and back'
jokes.punchline: Dana corrects the idiom: if it's 'to hell and back,' that's a return trip, not one-way
jokes.explanation: Another strong Dana literalism joke — correcting 'one-way trip to hell and back' on logical grounds is a good application of her character trait. The precision of 'that's actually a return' is perfect. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: But, you know, you're gonna have your bag forever, so what the fuck?
jokes.setup: The dinner is being cut short due to the crisis
jokes.punchline: Selina tries to frame the abandoned dinner positively by focusing on the bag gift — 'you're gonna have your bag forever, so what the fuck' — which is simultaneously dismissive and unexpectedly touching
jokes.explanation: An unexpected comedic grace note — 'you're gonna have your bag forever, so what the fuck?' is Selina at her most accidentally tender. The profanity deflates any sentimentality while the sentiment is genuinely there. Good economy and strong character integration. The callback to the bag gift as the compensating consolation prize is well-structured.

jokes.text: Who the hell does he think he is? / 'George Dennis' of 'Le Monde' called the vice president a typical American hick. / I think it's pronounced Georges Denis. / I'm not a hick. Screw you, Depardieu.
jokes.setup: The European press is calling Selina a 'typical American hick' over the song
jokes.punchline: 'Screw you, Depardieu' — Selina's retaliation against the French press via Gerard Depardieu, the most prominent French cultural export she can name
jokes.explanation: 'Screw you, Depardieu' is a great line — the rhyme (screw you/Depardieu) makes it feel like a prepared insult, but the choice of Depardieu (a specific, beloved/reviled French actor) as the target of VP-level diplomatic anger is absurd. Also proves she's the 'American hick' by going straight to celebrity insults. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I just liked the song so much. And I thought you wanted it out there, so I just put it out there.
jokes.setup: Selina discovers Jonah leaked the Kent song to Reddit/Tumblr
jokes.punchline: Jonah's guileless explanation — 'I just liked it so much' — as the sole motivation for causing an international incident
jokes.explanation: Jonah's innocent enthusiasm as the cause of diplomatic catastrophe is perfectly calibrated. 'I thought you wanted it out there' is the most Jonah possible misreading of the situation.

jokes.text: Get off the plane. / What? / Get off of the plane. / Ma'am, we're taxiing. / I don't give a shit. Get the fuck off my plane.
jokes.setup: Selina discovers Jonah posted the song while already on Air Force Two taxiing for takeoff
jokes.punchline: The escalating insistence that Jonah must leave the plane even while it's taxiing — the physical impossibility doesn't deter her
jokes.explanation: The escalating urgency of Selina demanding Jonah leave a taxiing aircraft, overriding basic aviation logic with pure rage, is well-executed. 'I don't give a shit' cutting through the practical objection is a good Selina moment.

jokes.text: I cannot believe that you put that out on Tumble. / No, Tumblr.
jokes.setup: Selina berates Jonah for the leak
jokes.punchline: In the middle of a genuine crisis, Jonah still corrects Selina's mispronunciation of 'Tumblr'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah character moment — his inability to let a mispronunciation slide even while being ejected from an aircraft under diplomatic crisis conditions tells you everything about him. The callback to the earlier 'Tumblr vs. Tumble' confusion is elegant.

jokes.text: All of your privileges are gonna be removed. / Okay, but not the parking. / Yeah, the parking. / Wait, what? You have parking? / Not anymore. / But I didn't even own a car. I just had to sign a two-year lease for the Nissan Cube.
jokes.setup: Selina strips Jonah's privileges as punishment
jokes.punchline: The parking — the object of the entire episode's status anxiety — is lost; Jonah's revelation that he leased a car specifically for this parking spot is the kicker
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural crown jewel and one of the series' best escalation callbacks. The entire episode has been threaded through with the West Exec parking as a symbol of status — opening cold open, Jonah's defiant 'crying in my car' speech, and now here: not only does Jonah lose the parking, but he has signed a two-year lease on a Nissan Cube specifically to have a car to park there. The Nissan Cube as the specific vehicle is inspired — it's the most unglamorous, awkward car imaginable, perfectly matched to Jonah's aspirational but pitiful status game. The structure earns a 9.5 — this is the whole episode's comic arc resolved in a single beat. The callback to joke 50 ('crying in my car in West Wing Exec parking') makes this even richer.

jokes.text: He should really be on suicide watch. / Yeah, make sure he goes through with it.
jokes.setup: Kent's assistant appears dejected after being sent to 'numbers camp'
jokes.punchline: 'Make sure he goes through with it' — Selina endorses the suicide, twisting the welfare concern into a death wish
jokes.explanation: A perfectly sharp dark joke. The structure is elegant: Dan makes a standard 'suicide watch' comment, Selina redirects the concern into an endorsement — 'make sure he goes through with it.' The reversal is swift and the cruelty is perfectly in character for both Selina (who deploys this kind of dark deflection) and the show's ethos. Economy approaches 9.5 — maximum impact per word. Earns 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: Numbers, they never end, do they? But that's what's good about them... they keep going.
jokes.setup: Kent watching someone depressed being led away
jokes.punchline: Kent's attempt at a consoling philosophical observation about numbers — completely tone-deaf, revealing he can only access concepts through data
jokes.explanation: An exceptional piece of Kent character comedy. 'Numbers, they never end, do they? But that's what's good about them... they keep going' is a hilariously robotic attempt at consolation that transforms the infinite nature of numbers into a reassurance. The delivery should be completely sincere — Kent isn't joking, he's trying to help, but the best he can do is abstract mathematical comfort. The character integration here approaches 9.5 — this is a uniquely Kent sentence. Earns 8.3 craft.

jokes.text: Post-Jonah departure — 'Better?' / 'It's all good.' / 'Totally fine.' / 'Great.' — followed by a long silence beat.
jokes.setup: Jonah has been ejected from the plane. Everyone reassures each other it's fine.
jokes.punchline: The increasingly insistent 'totally fine / great' followed by the silence — the question 'How long is this flight?' breaks it — reveals that everyone is slightly stunned.
jokes.explanation: The silence is the comedy — the evacuation of Jonah from the plane leaves a vacuum that the forced 'fine' signifiers can't fill. 'How long is this flight?' breaks the tension perfectly.

jokes.text: Amy: 'How long is this flight?'
jokes.setup: Post-Jonah ejection silence on Air Force Two, heading to Europe for a crisis.
jokes.punchline: The perfectly mundane question that breaks the silence — expressing existential dread about the journey ahead in the most banal possible terms.
jokes.explanation: Four words of profound understated dread. The mundane question about flight time carries the weight of everything that's gone wrong. Maximum economy — the joke is entirely in the timing and context.

jokes.text: Kent pointing at demographic data: 'That's the Latino vote, right? That thing?' — pointing at an object on a screen.
jokes.setup: Kent is showing Mike the Dream Metric data — presumably a sophisticated demographic breakdown.
jokes.punchline: Kent's vague pointer — 'That thing?' — for the Latino vote implies he sees demographics as undifferentiated blobs on a chart.
jokes.explanation: A brief but sharp moment — Kent's 'that thing' for a demographic group reveals both his clinical abstraction of voters and the irony of the 'Dream Metric' pretension. Subtle.

jokes.text: Pen. [beat] I leased a Nissan Cube from you the other day. What are my options if I don't need it anymore? Can I just give that back? / No? / [Sighs] Paper.
jokes.setup: Jonah has been ejected from Air Force Two and stripped of his parking; he goes to a Nissan dealership
jokes.punchline: Jonah's hopeful 'Can I just give that back? No?' — the absurdly naive belief that a car lease can simply be returned — followed by the defeated request for paper (to sign new paperwork)
jokes.explanation: A beautiful cold-scene coda. Jonah at the dealership alone, asking if he can simply 'give back' a leased car is perfectly pathetic. The structure of the scene — 'pen,' then the question, then the answer via silence, then 'paper' — is excellent minimalist comedy. The physical props (pen → paper for new horrible paperwork) frame Jonah's defeat elegantly. The callback to the parking/Cube joke makes this the formal ending of that subplot. One of the episode's strongest scenes.

tmdb_overview: Selina is rankled when the White House releases an unflattering photo of her. Mike and Kent bond over sailing; Jonah gets an executive parking space; Sue has a job interview. Selina takes steps to keep her staff happy as they prepare for her appearance at the Vic Allen dinner.
insight: Sustained cringe carries an 81-point episode on 2.46 jokes per minute—character work over volume.
